verses.text: Ten years after the call, Sarai is still barren. She offers Hagar, her Egyptian servant, to Abram — a cultural workaround. Maybe through her I can have children. Abram listens to Sarai. The plan is human, not divine.

Ten years after the call, Sarai is still barren. She offers Hagar, her Egyptian servant, to Abram — a cultural workaround. Maybe through her I can have children. Abram listens to Sarai. The plan is human, not divine.
Hagar conceives quickly. And the moment she does, the household balance breaks — she begins to look on her mistress with contempt. Two women are caught in a system neither of them designed.
Hagar conceives quickly. And the moment she does, the household balance breaks — she begins to look on her mistress with contempt. Two women are caught in a system neither of them designed.

Sarai blames Abram. Abram steps back: she is your servant, do as you wish. Sarai treats Hagar harshly. The pregnant woman flees into the wilderness rather than stay. Sometimes Scripture refuses to make any character look good.

Sarai blames Abram. Abram steps back: she is your servant, do as you wish. Sarai treats Hagar harshly. The pregnant woman flees into the wilderness rather than stay. Sometimes Scripture refuses to make any character look good.

The angel of the LORD finds Hagar at a desert spring. Hagar, Sarai's servant — God knows her name and her story. Where are you coming from, and where are you going? He asks not because He doesn't know, but to draw her out.

The angel of the LORD finds Hagar at a desert spring. Hagar, Sarai's servant — God knows her name and her story. Where are you coming from, and where are you going? He asks not because He doesn't know, but to draw her out.
Return to your mistress and submit yourself under her hand. This is hard counsel — going back to harshness. But God isn't abandoning her; He is preparing to bless her in place.

And then a promise meant for Abram's line is given to a runaway slave girl: I will multiply your descendants beyond counting. Name your son Ishmael — God hears — because the LORD has heard your affliction.

And then a promise meant for Abram's line is given to a runaway slave girl: I will multiply your descendants beyond counting. Name your son Ishmael — God hears — because the LORD has heard your affliction.

Her son will be a wild donkey of a man — fiercely independent, in conflict with his neighbors, dwelling in the presence of his kin. The blessing is unconventional but real. Hagar's line will not be small.
Hagar names God. She is the first person in Scripture to name Him: Thou God seest me. The Hebrew is El Roi — the God who sees. The woman no one sees finds the God who sees her.
The well is named Beer-lahai-roi — the well of the Living One who sees me. For generations after, anyone passing that well remembered: God meets people in the wilderness.

Hagar returns and bears Ishmael. Abram is 86 — eleven years since the original call, and the wrong son has been born first. The right son is still thirteen years away. God's timing is slower than ours, and never wrong.

Hagar returns and bears Ishmael. Abram is 86 — eleven years since the original call, and the wrong son has been born first. The right son is still thirteen years away. God's timing is slower than ours, and never wrong.
